title: Phase 7: Testing & Verification
description: Comprehensive testing: unit tests for pure modules (slug, registry, conflict), API integration tests for all endpoints, markdown round-trip fixture tests, and a manual test checklist. Tests should use Node's built-in test runner (node:test) or vitest (already have Vite). Each test suite can be written as soon as the module it tests is complete — tests don't need to wait for full integration.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Testing strategy: unit tests for pure modules (can be written as soon as each module is done), integration tests for the API (need all endpoints), round-trip tests for the editor (need the Tiptap component), manual tests for the full system (need everything). Each test suite should be independently runnable. Use vitest (already have Vite) for a consistent test runner.

title: Slug generation unit tests
description: Unit tests for the slug generation function. Cases: basic name ('unified_plan.md' → 'unified_plan'), special chars ('My Plan (v2).md' → 'my_plan_v2'), consecutive underscores collapse, leading/trailing underscores trimmed, empty result falls back to 'doc', .markdown extension handled, path with directories (only basename used), already-clean names pass through unchanged. Use vitest or node:test. Depends on slug generation module (the code under test).
close_reason: Implemented 10 slugify unit tests, all passing

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Slug generation is a pure function — these tests are trivially parallelizable with everything else. The test cases are specified in the unified plan. Pay special attention to edge cases: filenames that are ALL special characters, filenames with unicode, filenames with only an extension (.md → empty → 'doc').

title: Registry unit tests
description: Unit tests for the document registry. Cases: register a file and retrieve by slug, register same file twice returns same slug (dedup), register two files with same basename get different slugs (collision), list returns all registered docs, remove deletes from both primary and secondary index, register nonexistent file throws, register non-.md file throws. Use temp files in a test directory. Depends on registry module (the code under test).
close_reason: Implemented registry unit tests: register, dedup, collision, list, remove, error cases

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Registry tests need real temp files on disk (the registry validates file existence on registration). Create a temp directory in beforeEach, clean up in afterEach. Test the dedup behavior carefully — it's the most subtle part of the registry.

title: Conflict handling unit tests
description: Unit tests for the conflict module. Cases: (1) normal save — file mtime matches, write succeeds, mtime updated. (2) conflict save — modify file externally, save with old mtime, original renamed to _conflict.md, new content written. (3) repeated conflicts — first creates _conflict.md, second creates _conflict_1.md, third _conflict_2.md. (4) file deleted — save recreates file. (5) atomic write — verify .tmp file doesn't linger on success. (6) conflict path generation — existing _conflict.md leads to _conflict_1.md. Use real temp files, not mocks. Depends on conflict module (the code under test).
close_reason: Implemented 9 conflict handling unit tests, all passing

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Use real temp files, not mocks. The conflict module does actual fs operations — mocking would defeat the purpose. Test the full chain: write file, note mtime, modify file externally, save with old mtime, verify conflict rename happened, verify new content is in original path, verify old content is in conflict path.

title: API integration tests
description: Integration tests that spin up the Express server and test all endpoints via HTTP. Cases: POST /open with valid .md path → 200 + {url, slug}. POST /open with nonexistent path → 400. POST /open with .txt file → 400. POST /open same file twice → same slug. GET /api/doc/:slug → 200 + {content, mtime}. GET /api/doc/nonexistent → 404. PUT /api/doc/:slug with matching mtime → 200 + {saved: true, conflict: false}. PUT /api/doc/:slug with stale mtime → 200 + {saved: true, conflict: true}. DELETE /api/doc/:slug → 200. GET /api/docs → array of registered docs. GET /health → {ok: true}. Use supertest or direct fetch against a test server instance. Depends on all endpoint implementations being complete.
close_reason: API integration tests: health, open, doc load, save+conflict, delete, list — all with Tailscale filter active

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Integration tests that boot a real Express server instance (on a random port to avoid conflicts with a running instance). Use supertest or fetch. These tests exercise the full stack: HTTP → routes → registry → filesystem. They should cover both success paths and error paths (404, 400, etc.).

title: Markdown round-trip fixture tests
description: Automated tests that verify the editor's markdown round-trip fidelity using fixture files. Similar to the spike but integrated into the test suite and covering edge cases found during spike. Load various .md fixtures, parse into Tiptap, serialize back, verify semantic preservation. Focus on the constructs used in real plans: requirement tables, task lists, code examples, nested lists. These tests serve as regression guards — if a Tiptap upgrade breaks round-tripping, these fail. Depends on the editor component (uses the same Tiptap configuration).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: These are REGRESSION GUARDS. If a Tiptap package update breaks markdown round-tripping, these tests catch it. They serve a different purpose than the spike: the spike is a one-time go/no-go decision, these tests are ongoing protection. Use the same fixtures but integrate them into the standard test suite.

title: Manual test execution checklist
description: Execute the full manual test checklist with a running server. This is the final validation before declaring V1 complete. Tests: (1) curl POST /open with a real .md file, get URL back. (2) Open URL in browser via Tailscale — verify editor loads with rendered markdown. (3) Verify single-surface editing (no split pane). (4) Edit a table: add/remove rows, edit cells. (5) Edit a code block: verify code stays in block. (6) Wait for autosave, verify file updated on disk. (7) Modify file on disk externally, trigger autosave from browser → verify _conflict rename. (8) Register 3+ docs from different terminals → verify all coexist. (9) Visit index page → verify all docs listed with links. (10) Attempt access from non-Tailscale IP → verify 403. (11) Kill and restart server → verify docs are gone. Depends on SPA serving, index page, autosave, and network spike all being complete — this tests the fully integrated system.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: This is the FINAL VALIDATION before declaring V1 complete. It tests the fully integrated system end-to-end, including things automated tests can't cover: does the editor FEEL right? Is the Tailscale URL accessible from outside the VPS? Does the conflict notification make sense to a human? Run through every item on the checklist and record results.

title: Phase 1: Server Infrastructure
description: Build the server skeleton: Tailscale hostname resolution, Express server on port 7979, network filtering middleware, and health endpoint. The server is the backbone that everything else plugs into. Fail-fast philosophy: if Tailscale hostname can't be resolved or port 7979 is taken, crash immediately with a clear error. No fallbacks. No silent degradation.
close_reason: Completed server infrastructure: tailscale resolver, network filter + spike validation, Express skeleton with /health and startup banner.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: PHILOSOPHY: Fail-fast everywhere. The user explicitly said 'no fallbacks — fallbacks are satan — loud failures.' If Tailscale can't be resolved: crash. If port 7979 is taken: crash. If network filtering can't be verified: stop and report. This is dev-stage software, not production with SLAs. Clear errors beat silent degradation.

title: Tailscale hostname resolution module (tailscale.ts)
description: Create src/server/tailscale.ts. Runs 'tailscale status --json' via child_process.execSync, parses JSON, extracts Self.DNSName, strips trailing dot. Caches result in module scope. Exports getTailscaleHostname(): string. If tailscale command fails or DNSName is empty, throw an error that crashes the process with a clear message. Already verified on this machine: returns 'flywheel.tail2a835b.ts.net'. This is a standalone module with no server dependencies — it just needs TypeScript and @types/node (from bootstrap).
close_reason: Added tailscale status resolver with caching + error handling.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: VERIFIED: 'tailscale status --json' works on this machine and returns Self.DNSName = 'flywheel.tail2a835b.ts.net.' (note trailing dot — must strip it). The user said the hostname changes 'almost never' so caching at startup is correct. Do NOT re-resolve on every request. The user specifically said: 'it should just check it every time the server itself launches and then hold it in memory.'

title: Network filtering middleware (network.ts)
description: Create src/server/network.ts. Express middleware that checks req.socket.remoteAddress against Tailscale CGNAT range (100.64.0.0/10) and localhost (127.0.0.1, ::1). In --dev mode (detected via NODE_ENV or CLI flag), skip filtering entirely. In production, reject non-matching requests with 403 Forbidden and a clear JSON error body. This is a pure middleware function — it does not depend on the Express server existing, just on Express types being installed. The CGNAT range is hardcoded (it's a Tailscale constant), so this does NOT depend on the tailscale module.
close_reason: Added tailscale-only middleware with dev-mode bypass and CGNAT range checks.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: DESIGN DECISION: The Tailscale CGNAT range 100.64.0.0/10 is a constant defined by Tailscale. We also need to allow 127.0.0.1 and ::1 for localhost access (agents run curl locally). In dev mode (NODE_ENV=development), skip filtering entirely — the user needs unrestricted access during development. The user's exact words: 'just reject any traffic outside the current tailscale network' but also 'no tokens or anything' — so this is the ONLY security layer.

title: Express server entry point + health endpoint (index.ts)
description: Create src/server/index.ts. Creates Express app, applies JSON body parser, applies network middleware, binds to 0.0.0.0:7979. Calls getTailscaleHostname() at startup to get the hostname (TRUE dependency — the server needs this for URL construction and startup banner). Adds GET /health returning {ok: true, tailscaleHost, docCount: 0, uptime}. Prints startup banner with Tailscale URL. If bind fails (EADDRINUSE), crash with clear error. Does NOT depend on network middleware at this stage — middleware can be wired in later; the server skeleton just needs to boot. NOTE: This depends on tailscale module because it calls getTailscaleHostname() during startup, and the returned hostname is stored in server state for use by all route handlers that construct URLs.
close_reason: Added Express server skeleton with /health, Tailscale URL banner, and EADDRINUSE handling.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: WHY THIS DEPENDS ON TAILSCALE: The server stores the Tailscale hostname at startup and uses it in every POST /open response to construct the full URL (http://<host>:7979/doc/<slug>). Without the hostname, the server cannot fulfill its primary purpose. The health endpoint also returns the hostname. The startup banner also displays it. Three consumers, one module-level call at boot.

title: Network restriction spike — validate filtering works on this VPS
description: With the Express server running and network middleware written, wire the middleware into the server and test: (1) curl from localhost in dev mode — should work, (2) curl from Tailscale IP in prod mode — should work, (3) attempt from non-Tailscale source in prod mode — should get 403. Document the results. If filtering doesn't work reliably (e.g., remoteAddress is always ::ffff:127.0.0.1 behind a proxy), stop and document why. Do not ship an unverified security boundary. This depends on both the server (needs a running Express app to test against) and the middleware (the code being validated).
close_reason: Validated dev bypass + prod filtering; documented results in spike/network-filtering.md.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: WHY A SPIKE: The user's requirement to 'reject traffic outside the current tailscale network' is clear in intent but technically ambiguous. req.socket.remoteAddress behavior depends on whether the request arrives via IPv4, IPv6, or through a proxy. On some VPS setups, all local traffic shows as ::ffff:127.0.0.1. This spike validates that our middleware ACTUALLY works on THIS specific VPS before we ship it. If it doesn't work, we document why and escalate — we don't silently skip security.

title: Phase 3: Document Registry
description: Build the in-memory document registry that maps URL slugs to absolute file paths. This is the core data structure the entire system revolves around — agents register files, the registry assigns slugs, and all subsequent operations (load, save, serve) look up documents by slug. Must handle: slug generation from filenames, collision resolution, dedup by absolute path, CRUD operations. Optionally backed by JSON for restart recovery. This is pure business logic with no HTTP or UI dependencies.
close_reason: All children completed: types, slugify, registry CRUD, JSON persistence

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: The registry is the central data structure. It maps slugs to file paths. Every HTTP operation (open, load, save, delete, list) goes through it. The user's requirements for slugs: use filename (not numeric ID), strip extension, replace bad chars with underscore, handle collisions with _1/_2 suffix. The user was explicit: 'the document ID should be really short' and 'even better than an ID number would just be the file name.'

title: Shared TypeScript types (types.ts)
description: Create src/shared/types.ts defining the core interfaces used by both server and client: RegistryEntry {slug, absolutePath, originalBaseName, registeredAt, lastSavedAt, lastKnownMtimeMs, lastKnownSize}, OpenRequest {path: string}, OpenResponse {url, slug}, DocResponse {content, mtime, slug, fileName}, SaveRequest {content, baseMtime}, SaveResponse {saved, conflict, mtime, conflictPath?, recreated?}, HealthResponse {ok, tailscaleHost, docCount, uptime}. These types are the contract between server and client — defining them first ensures both sides agree on shapes. No runtime dependencies, just TypeScript.
close_reason: Already implemented by another agent

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: These types are the API contract between server and client. Defining them FIRST means both sides can be developed against the same interfaces even when built in parallel by different agents. The RegistryEntry type also documents what metadata we track — future agents can see at a glance what data is available for each document.

title: Slug generation logic
description: Implement slug generation as a pure function in src/server/registry.ts (or a separate slugify.ts if preferred). Algorithm: (1) path.basename(filePath, '.md') — also handle .markdown, (2) lowercase, (3) replace any char not in [a-z0-9_-] with underscore, (4) collapse consecutive underscores to one, (5) trim leading/trailing underscores, (6) if empty after sanitization use 'doc'. This is a pure function with no side effects — it does not check the registry for collisions (that's the registry's job). Test cases: 'unified_plan.md' → 'unified_plan', 'My Plan (v2).md' → 'my_plan_v2', '___weird___name___.md' → 'weird_name', '.md' → 'doc'.
close_reason: Completed — slugify.ts implements all spec requirements: basename extraction (.md/.markdown), lowercase, char replacement, underscore collapsing, trim, empty fallback to doc

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: SLUG ALGORITHM is specified in the unified plan: basename → lowercase → replace non-[a-z0-9_-] with _ → collapse consecutive _ → trim leading/trailing _ → fallback to 'doc' if empty. This is a PURE FUNCTION — it takes a filename and returns a slug. It does NOT check for collisions (that's the registry's job). Keeping it pure makes it trivially testable. The user said: 'just replace any incompatible character with like an underscore or something. It doesn't matter. We're not using this name to do anything. It's just for visual differentiation.'

title: In-memory registry with CRUD operations
description: Implement the document registry in src/server/registry.ts. Uses two Maps: primary Map<slug, RegistryEntry> and secondary Map<absolutePath, slug> for O(1) dedup lookups. API: registerDocument(filePath) — canonicalizes path via path.resolve(), checks secondary index for dedup (returns existing if found), generates slug via slugify, resolves collisions by appending _1/_2/etc, stats file for initial mtime/size, creates RegistryEntry, stores in both maps, returns entry. getDocument(slug), getDocumentByPath(absPath), listDocuments(), removeDocument(slug) — removes from both maps. The registry validates that the file exists and is .md/.markdown on registration. Throws on nonexistent/non-md files. Depends on slug generation (calls it) and shared types (uses RegistryEntry interface).
close_reason: Already implemented by GreenMeadow

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: TWO MAPS for O(1) lookups both ways: Map<slug, RegistryEntry> (primary, for HTTP lookups by slug) and Map<absolutePath, slug> (secondary, for dedup — when a second agent registers the same file, return the existing slug instead of creating a duplicate). The user said multiple agents may register files simultaneously. Same-file dedup prevents slug proliferation. Use path.resolve() to canonicalize before dedup comparison.

title: Optional JSON persistence for registry
description: Add optional JSON persistence to the registry. On every mutation (register, remove), serialize the full registry to ~/.plan-present/registry.json. On startup, if the file exists, load it and revalidate every entry: stat each absolutePath, discard entries where the file no longer exists. This is lower priority than core functionality — the user said re-registering via curl is acceptable. But it improves the restart experience when the user has many documents open. Depends on the registry module being complete since it extends its behavior.
close_reason: Implemented JSON persistence to ~/.plan-present/registry.json with disk load/save and startup restore

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: PRIORITY: LOW. The user acknowledged that re-registering via curl is trivial. JSON persistence is a nice-to-have for restart recovery but NOT blocking for V1. The unified plan says: 'implement JSON persistence if time allows, but it's not blocking.' If this task is still open when everything else is done, do it. If time is tight, skip it.

title: Phase 5: Autosave & Conflict Handling
description: Implement the complete save pipeline: client-side autosave with debounce, server-side conflict detection, atomic file writes, and the conflict rename protocol. The user specified: autosave with debounce, browser copy is authoritative on conflict, rename on-disk original to *_conflict.md. This phase contains both server-side (conflict module, save endpoint) and client-side (autosave hook, beforeunload) work. The conflict module is pure file-system logic that can be built in parallel with everything else.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: The user specified: 'auto-save is compatible with the above, let's do it or use debounce, whatever.' The conflict protocol is the critical piece: 'rename the original by appending _conflict to its name and assume the user temp copy is authoritative.' Browser copy ALWAYS wins. The on-disk file gets renamed, never the browser version. This is a deliberate design choice — the user is actively editing in the browser, so their work takes priority over background changes.

title: Conflict resolution module (conflict.ts)
description: Create src/server/conflict.ts — pure file-system logic for conflict handling, fully independent of Express or the registry. Exports: generateConflictPath(originalPath): string — given '/path/to/plan.md', checks if 'plan_conflict.md' exists; if so tries 'plan_conflict_1.md', 'plan_conflict_2.md', etc. Always preserves .md extension. Returns the first available conflict path. atomicWrite(filePath, content): void — writes content to filePath.tmp then fs.renameSync to filePath (atomic on same filesystem). saveWithConflictDetection(filePath, content, expectedMtimeMs): {saved, conflict, conflictPath?, mtime, recreated?} — the main save function. Stats the file, compares mtime, either writes normally (atomic) or triggers conflict rename then writes. Handles file-deleted-on-disk case (just recreates). This module has ZERO dependencies beyond Node.js fs/path — it can be built immediately after bootstrap, in parallel with everything else.
close_reason: Implemented conflict.ts with generateConflictPath, atomicWrite, saveWithConflictDetection

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: ZERO DEPENDENCIES beyond Node.js fs/path. This is pure file-system logic. It can be built immediately after bootstrap, in parallel with everything else. The conflict naming algorithm: plan.md → plan_conflict.md → plan_conflict_1.md → plan_conflict_2.md. Always preserve .md extension. Always check existence before choosing a name. The atomicWrite function (write .tmp, rename) prevents partial writes from corrupting files.

title: Save API endpoint — PUT /api/doc/:slug
description: Add PUT /api/doc/:slug route to Express. Receives {content: string, baseMtime: number}. Looks up slug in registry (404 if not found). Calls saveWithConflictDetection(entry.absolutePath, content, baseMtime). Updates registry entry's lastSavedAt and lastKnownMtimeMs. Returns SaveResponse per shared types. This is where three independent modules converge: the Express server (provides the HTTP layer), the registry (provides the slug-to-path lookup), and the conflict module (provides the actual save logic). All three are TRUE dependencies — this endpoint cannot be written without them.
close_reason: PUT /api/doc/:slug with conflict detection, registry mtime update

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONVERGENCE POINT: This is where three independently-built modules meet — Express server (HTTP layer), registry (slug-to-path mapping), and conflict module (save logic). All three are true dependencies: the endpoint cannot be implemented without knowing how to find the file path (registry), how to save safely (conflict module), or how to receive HTTP requests (Express). This is the most dependency-heavy task in the project.

title: Document load endpoint — GET /api/doc/:slug
description: Add GET /api/doc/:slug route to Express. Looks up slug in registry (404 if not found). Reads the file from disk (fs.readFile), stats for mtime. Returns DocResponse: {content, mtime, slug, fileName: entry.originalBaseName}. If file has been deleted since registration, return 404 with clear error message. Depends on Express server (HTTP layer) and registry (slug lookup).
close_reason: GET /api/doc/:slug already implemented

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Simple endpoint but important: this is what the browser editor calls to get the initial document content. Returns the raw markdown string plus the current file mtime (used as baseMtime by the autosave hook for conflict detection). If the file has been deleted since registration, return 404 — don't silently return empty content.

title: Registration & management endpoints — POST /open, DELETE, GET /api/docs
description: Add three routes: (1) POST /open — receives {path: string}, validates path is absolute, calls registry.registerDocument(), constructs URL using cached tailscale hostname, returns OpenResponse {url, slug}. This is THE agent-facing endpoint — it's what every AI agent calls via curl. (2) DELETE /api/doc/:slug — calls registry.removeDocument(), returns {removed: true} or 404. (3) GET /api/docs — calls registry.listDocuments(), returns array of {slug, fileName, url} for all registered docs. Depends on Express server (HTTP layer, hostname) and registry (document operations).
close_reason: POST /open, DELETE /api/doc/:slug, GET /api/docs endpoints implemented

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: POST /open is THE agent-facing endpoint. It's what every AI agent calls via curl. The curl command agents use: curl -s -X POST http://localhost:7979/open -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"path": "/absolute/path/to/plan.md"}'. The response includes the full Tailscale URL that the agent passes back to the user. This is the entire MCP-replacement interface — one curl command, one JSON response.

title: Client autosave hook (useAutosave.ts)
description: Create src/client/useAutosave.ts — custom React hook. Takes the Tiptap editor instance and slug as inputs. Watches editor for changes via editor.on('update'). Debounces saves: 2 seconds after last change. On trigger: serializes content to Markdown via editor storage, PUTs to /api/doc/{slug} with {content, baseMtime}. On success: updates baseMtime from response mtime, sets status to 'saved'. On conflict: updates baseMtime, sets status to conflict message including conflictPath. On network error: sets status to error, will retry on next edit (not immediately — avoid retry storms). Returns {status, isSaving, lastSavedAt} for the status indicator to consume. Depends on editor component because it needs the Tiptap editor instance and its Markdown serialization API.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: DEBOUNCE TIMING: 2 seconds after last keystroke. This is a sweet spot — fast enough that the user rarely loses more than a few seconds of work, slow enough to avoid hammering the server during active typing. The hook must track a 'dirty' flag independently of the debounce timer for the beforeunload handler to check. The Markdown serialization uses Tiptap's editor.storage.markdown.getMarkdown() or equivalent API — must match whatever the spike validated.

title: beforeunload save handler
description: Add beforeunload event handler in the autosave hook or Editor component. On page close/navigate: if there are unsaved changes (dirty flag from debounce state), trigger an immediate synchronous save using navigator.sendBeacon() or a synchronous XHR (sendBeacon preferred — it survives page unload). Also set event.returnValue to trigger the browser's 'unsaved changes' confirmation dialog. This prevents data loss when the user closes a tab mid-edit. Depends on the autosave hook because it extends that hook's functionality and uses its internal state (dirty flag, current content).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: LAST LINE OF DEFENSE against data loss. If the user closes the tab mid-edit, this handler fires. Use navigator.sendBeacon() for the save request — it's designed to survive page unload, unlike fetch() or XHR. Also set event.returnValue = 'You have unsaved changes' to trigger the browser's built-in confirmation dialog. Note: modern browsers show their own generic message, not our custom text.

title: Phase 2: Tiptap Markdown Spike (CRITICAL GATE)
description: Validate that Tiptap can round-trip the Markdown constructs used in implementation plans BEFORE building the editor. This is a HARD GATE — if tables don't round-trip, STOP and escalate to user. Known risks: GitHub issue #5750 (table export fails), #7435 (table syntax not supported as input), getMarkdown() returning empty strings. The spike must produce a written VERDICT.md with pass/fail evidence for every construct. This phase has NO dependency on server infrastructure — it only needs npm packages installed.
close_reason: Spike completed; VERDICT.md shows table round-trip failure (tables removed). Gate failed; STOP and escalate.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: THIS IS THE HIGHEST-RISK PHASE. Research (March 2026) found: GitHub #5750 — Tiptap table export to markdown broken. GitHub #7435 — markdown table syntax not supported as direct input. getMarkdown() sometimes returns empty strings. If tables don't round-trip, the ENTIRE product is compromised because implementation plans are TABLE-HEAVY. The spike must produce irrefutable evidence before we invest in the editor. No wishful thinking.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: LOCKED by codex (agent) — starting spike verification.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Spike already executed. spike/VERDICT.md (2026-03-28) shows tables removed on round-trip (FAIL). Gate failed; stop and escalate. Evidence: requirements + mixed-sequence tables both removed; other constructs PASS.

title: Create sample markdown fixture (spike/sample.md)
description: Author spike/sample.md — a representative implementation plan containing every Markdown construct we need to support: H1/H2/H3 headings, paragraphs with bold/italic/inline code/links, nested bullet lists, nested numbered lists, task lists (- [ ] and - [x]), GFM tables (simple 2-col and wider multi-col with varied content), fenced code blocks with language tags (bash), blockquotes (single and nested), horizontal rules, and mixed sequences (table after list, heading after code block). This file is the test fixture — it should look like a REAL implementation plan, not a synthetic test. Use content that mirrors the unified_plan.md structure.
close_reason: Authored spike/sample.md with full Markdown construct coverage.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: The fixture must be REALISTIC, not synthetic. Use the structure of a real implementation plan: requirements table, task breakdown table, status table, code examples, nested lists of steps, task list checkboxes. The unified_plan.md in this very project is a good template — it contains all the constructs we need to test. Consider copying a section of it as the fixture.

title: Implement spike script (spike/table-roundtrip.ts)
description: Create spike/table-roundtrip.ts — a Node.js script that programmatically tests Tiptap Markdown round-tripping WITHOUT a browser. Use Tiptap's headless/Node APIs (or jsdom if needed). Configure the EXACT same extensions planned for the real editor: StarterKit, Table/TableRow/TableCell/TableHeader, TaskList/TaskItem, CodeBlockLowlight, Markdown with GFM. Load sample.md as string, parse into Tiptap document, serialize back to Markdown. For each construct, compare input vs output and classify as PASS (identical), NORMALIZED (formatting changed but semantics preserved), or FAIL (content/structure loss). Print a structured report. Special attention to tables: are cell contents preserved? Is structure (rows x cols) preserved? Is alignment preserved? Add 'spike' script to package.json to run this.
close_reason: Implemented spike/table-roundtrip.ts to parse+serialize markdown and report PASS/NORMALIZED/FAIL per construct.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CRITICAL: This script must use the EXACT SAME Tiptap extension configuration as the real editor. If the spike uses different extensions than the editor, the verdict is invalid. Import the same extension list. The spike should run headless (no browser) using Tiptap's Node.js APIs. If Tiptap requires a DOM, use jsdom. The output must be machine-parseable AND human-readable — future agents need to be able to grep for PASS/FAIL/NORMALIZED.

title: Execute spike, write VERDICT.md, gate decision
description: Run the spike script against sample.md. Write results to spike/VERDICT.md with: (1) date and package versions tested, (2) per-construct verdict table (construct | input snippet | output snippet | verdict), (3) overall assessment. Decision criteria: ALL PASS or NORMALIZED → proceed to Phase 4 editor work. Any FAIL on tables (content loss, structure loss) → STOP. Escalate to user. Do NOT switch editors, do NOT build workarounds, do NOT proceed. Present the evidence. Any FAIL on other constructs → evaluate severity against real plan documents, escalate if it affects usability. This bead depends on BOTH the sample fixture AND the spike script being complete.
close_reason: PASS. Tables round-trip after fixing imports (default→named). VERDICT.md updated. Proceed to Phase 4.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: GATE RULES: (1) If all constructs PASS or NORMALIZED → green light for Phase 4. (2) If tables FAIL with content/structure loss → FULL STOP. Do not switch to Milkdown. Do not build custom serializers. Do not proceed. Escalate to the user with the exact evidence. The user explicitly chose Tiptap and explicitly rejected fallbacks. (3) If non-table constructs FAIL → evaluate against real plan documents. Minor formatting normalization is acceptable. Semantic loss is not.

title: Phase 0: Project Bootstrap
description: Initialize the Node.js/TypeScript project with all dependencies, build tooling, directory structure, and scripts. This is the root of the entire dependency graph — every other task requires a working project scaffold with installed packages before it can begin. Goal: 'npm run build' succeeds with an empty app.
close_reason: Bootstrapped empty app: added minimal server/client entrypoints and verified npm run build succeeds.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: This is a greenfield Node.js/TypeScript project. The VPS has Node v25.8.0, npm 11.11.0, Python 3.14, and Tailscale running. The project directory already has .gitignore, .vscode/settings.json, and sprint files, but zero application code. All dependencies must be installed from scratch. The user's editor preference (tabSize: 2, formatOnSave) is already set in .vscode/settings.json.

title: npm init, install all dependencies, create package.json scripts
description: Run npm init -y. Install ALL dependencies in one shot (server: express, @types/express; client: react, react-dom, @types/react, @types/react-dom, @tiptap/core, @tiptap/react, @tiptap/pm, @tiptap/starter-kit, @tiptap/markdown, @tiptap/extension-table, @tiptap/extension-table-row, @tiptap/extension-table-cell, @tiptap/extension-table-header, @tiptap/extension-task-list, @tiptap/extension-task-item, @tiptap/extension-code-block-lowlight, lowlight; build: typescript, @types/node, tsx, vite, @vitejs/plugin-react). Add scripts: dev, build, start, spike. Verify npm run build succeeds.
close_reason: Installed deps + scripts; Tiptap packages pinned to exact 3.21.0. npm run build currently fails until tsconfig.server.json/Vite client scaffold exist (nj9.2/tlx.1).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: IMPORTANT: Install ALL deps in one shot to avoid repeated npm install calls. The package list is finalized in the unified plan. Use exact versions (not ranges) for Tiptap packages to avoid the known instability noted in the risk assessment. The spike and the editor MUST use identical Tiptap versions — if they diverge, the spike's verdict is meaningless.

title: Create TypeScript configs and directory structure
description: Create tsconfig.json (base config with strict mode), tsconfig.server.json (extends base, Node target, includes src/server/** and src/shared/**), vite.config.ts (React plugin, output to dist/client). Create directories: src/server/, src/client/, src/shared/, spike/. Update .gitignore to add node_modules/ and dist/. This depends on T0_1 because tsconfig needs to reference installed @types packages and vite.config needs the vite package.
close_reason: Added tsconfig base/server configs, vite config, and created src/ + spike directories. .gitignore already had node_modules/dist.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: RATIONALE: Two tsconfig files are needed because the server targets Node.js (CommonJS or ESM with Node resolution) while the client is bundled by Vite (ESM, browser target). The base tsconfig.json has shared strict settings. tsconfig.server.json extends it with Node-specific paths. Vite handles client TS compilation via its own pipeline. The shared/ directory should be included in BOTH configs since types.ts is imported by both server and client code.

title: Phase 4: Browser Editor
description: Build the Tiptap-based WYSIWYG editor served in the browser. This is the core user-facing feature — the entire project exists to provide this editing experience. Single-surface rendering (no split pane), Obsidian-inspired styling, keyboard-driven formatting. The editor component MUST NOT be started until the Tiptap spike (Phase 2) passes — the spike determines whether the chosen extensions actually work for our use case. The SPA shell (React + Vite scaffolding) CAN start in parallel with the spike since it's just boilerplate.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: THIS IS THE CORE USER EXPERIENCE. The entire project exists to provide this editing surface. The user's exact words: 'while editing it, I would still like it to all be getting rendered based on the style defined by the markdown itself. It's WYSIWYG. But without using an editing panel and a rendering panel separately.' The user rejected TOAST UI (split pane) and chose Tiptap specifically for single-surface WYSIWYG. Obsidian was the UX reference point.

title: React SPA shell + Vite config (index.html, App.tsx)
description: Create src/client/index.html (minimal HTML mounting a React root), src/client/main.tsx (React entry point), src/client/App.tsx (extracts slug from window.location.pathname matching /doc/:slug pattern, renders <Editor slug={slug} /> or an error if no slug). Ensure vite.config.ts is configured correctly for React with the @vitejs/plugin-react plugin. Verify: npm run dev starts Vite dev server and serves the shell page. This is pure React/Vite scaffolding with NO Tiptap code — it can be done in parallel with the spike.
close_reason: Implemented slug extraction from /doc/:slug in App.tsx, Editor component placeholder, error state for missing slug. Vite config already correct.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: This is pure React/Vite scaffolding — NO Tiptap code. It can be built in parallel with the spike because it doesn't depend on Tiptap working. It just sets up the SPA container that the editor component will mount into. The slug extraction from URL path is the only logic: parse window.location.pathname to get the slug from /doc/<slug>.

title: Tiptap editor component (Editor.tsx)
description: Create src/client/Editor.tsx — the core WYSIWYG editor. On mount: GET /api/doc/{slug}, receive {content, mtime, fileName}. Store baseMtime in state. Initialize Tiptap useEditor() with extensions: StarterKit (headings, paragraphs, bold, italic, lists, code blocks, blockquotes, horizontal rules), Table + TableRow + TableCell + TableHeader, TaskList + TaskItem, CodeBlockLowlight configured with lowlight (syntax highlighting), Markdown extension with GFM enabled. Parse fetched Markdown into editor via setContent. Display fileName in a subtle header bar above the editor. The editor surface should fill the viewport below the header. No toolbar — formatting via keyboard shortcuts only (Ctrl+B, Ctrl+I, etc. provided by StarterKit). This DEPENDS ON the spike verdict (T2_VERDICT) because: the spike determines whether our chosen Tiptap extension configuration actually works. If the spike reveals that e.g. tables need a different extension or config, this component must use that revised config. The spike's output is a direct input to this component's design.
close_reason: Implemented Tiptap editor with StarterKit, Table, TaskList, CodeBlockLowlight, Markdown extensions using named imports per spike verdict

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: WHY THIS DEPENDS ON THE SPIKE: The spike determines whether our chosen extension configuration actually works. If tables need a different extension or a workaround, this component must incorporate that. The spike's VERDICT.md is a direct input to this component's design. Without the spike passing, building this component risks wasted work on a broken foundation. This is not a conceptual dependency — the spike's output literally determines the extension list and configuration used here.

title: Editor CSS styling — Obsidian-inspired (style.css)
description: Create src/client/style.css. Obsidian-inspired: clean, readable, minimal chrome. System font stack (ui-sans-serif, system-ui, etc.), line-height 1.6, comfortable font size (16-18px). Headings: visually distinct by size and weight, subtle bottom border on H1/H2. Code blocks: monospace font (ui-monospace, Consolas, etc.), subtle gray background (#f5f5f5), rounded corners, 1em padding. Tables: visible cell borders (1px solid #ddd), subtle header row background (#f9f9f9), comfortable cell padding (8px 12px). Blockquotes: 3px left border accent (blue-gray), slight indent, italic. Task lists: actual checkbox inputs. Links: colored (#0066cc), underlined on hover. Full viewport height editor with a small header bar showing filename. Save status indicator: small fixed text in bottom-right corner. Depends on editor component because the CSS targets Tiptap's generated DOM structure (ProseMirror class names like .ProseMirror, .ProseMirror table, etc.).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: TARGETS TIPTAP DOM STRUCTURE: Tiptap/ProseMirror generates specific CSS classes (.ProseMirror for the root, standard HTML elements inside). The CSS must target these. Reference Obsidian's aesthetic: clean typography, visible structure, minimal chrome. The user views these plans on a regular monitor via browser — optimize for desktop readability, not mobile. No dark mode unless the user asks for it later.

title: Save status indicator UI component
description: Add a small, non-intrusive save status indicator to the editor page. Fixed position bottom-right corner. States: 'Saved' (green, fades after 2s), 'Saving...' (gray, animated), 'Conflict detected — original saved as <name>' (orange, persists until next save), 'Error saving' (red, persists). The component receives status via props or context from the autosave hook. Depends on editor component because it's embedded in the editor page layout.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: This is a small but important UX element. The user needs to know: is my work saved? The indicator should be subtle (not a modal or toast) but visible. Fixed position bottom-right. Color-coded: green=saved, gray=saving, orange=conflict, red=error. The conflict state should mention the conflict filename so the user knows where the backup is.

title: Phase 6: Integration & Polish
description: Wire everything together: serve the built SPA from Express, create the index page, finalize the startup banner, configure dev/prod modes. This phase is about connecting independently-built components into a working whole. Most tasks here depend on multiple prior phases converging.
close_reason: All 4 children completed: SPA serving, index page, startup banner, dev/prod mode

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: This phase wires independently-built components into a working whole. The SPA serving, index page, startup banner, and dev/prod scripts are all integration work that depends on prior phases. Most of these are quick tasks — the hard work was done in phases 1-5.

title: SPA serving from Express — GET /doc/:slug
description: Configure Express to serve the Vite-built SPA for all /doc/:slug routes. In production: express.static('dist/client') for assets, catch-all for /doc/* that serves dist/client/index.html. In dev mode: proxy to Vite dev server or use vite-express middleware. The SPA reads the slug from the URL path client-side. Also verify slug exists in registry before serving (return 404 HTML if unknown slug). Depends on Express server (the app to configure) and SPA shell (the built assets to serve).
close_reason: SPA serving for /doc/:slug with production static files and dev redirect to Vite

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: In production: Vite builds the SPA to dist/client/, Express serves it with express.static(). For /doc/:slug routes, serve index.html (SPA routing). In dev: either use vite-express to embed Vite middleware into Express, or run Vite on a separate port (5173) and configure CORS. The dev approach is a practical choice — pick whichever is simpler to set up.

title: Index page — GET /
description: Create GET / route that serves a simple HTML page listing all registered documents. Server-rendered HTML (no React needed — this is a simple list). Each entry shows: document filename, slug, clickable link to /doc/<slug>, registration timestamp. If no documents registered, show a helpful message explaining how to register via curl. Include the curl example command in the empty state. The page can be static (generated on each request from registry.listDocuments()) — no need for live updates. Depends on registration endpoints because those endpoints populate the registry that this page reads, and the GET /api/docs endpoint provides the data.
close_reason: Implemented GET / index page with empty state + curl example and document listing table

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: This page is a convenience for the user to see what documents are currently registered. It's server-rendered HTML (not React) because it's a simple list and doesn't need client-side interactivity. The empty state should include the curl example command — this helps new users (or the user's future self) remember how to register a document.

title: Startup banner and logging
description: Enhance the server startup output. Print a clear banner showing: server name/version, port, full Tailscale URL, current mode (dev/production). Add minimal request logging middleware: log method, path, status code, response time in ms. One line per request. Use console.log, not a logging framework. The banner is important because the user launches this in tmux and needs to see the URL immediately. Depends on Express server (the startup code where the banner is printed).
close_reason: Added startup banner with name/version/port/mode/URL, plus per-request logging middleware (method, path, status, ms)

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: The user launches this in a tmux session and needs to immediately see the URL. The banner should be impossible to miss. Include the full http:// URL so it can be cmd-clicked in most terminals. Also include the current mode (dev/production) so the user knows whether network filtering is active.

title: Dev/prod mode configuration
description: Finalize the two run modes. 'npm start': NODE_ENV=production, serves pre-built client from dist/client, Tailscale-only network filtering active, runs compiled JS from dist/server. 'npm run dev': NODE_ENV=development, Vite HMR for client (either vite-express integration or separate Vite dev server on another port with CORS), network filter disabled (all traffic allowed), server runs via tsx with watch mode for auto-restart on changes. Ensure package.json scripts are correct. Test both modes boot successfully. Depends on Express server and SPA shell (both must exist to configure how they're served).
close_reason: Configured explicit NODE_ENV in npm scripts, excluded tests from server build, fixed dist path, verified both modes boot

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Two modes serve different needs: dev mode is for building the tool itself (hot reload, all traffic allowed, fast iteration). Production mode is for actual use (pre-built assets, Tailscale-only, stable). The user will primarily use production mode once V1 is built, but dev mode is essential during implementation.
